children.value: We can construct an approximate trial wavefunction for a molecule using atomic orbitals to gain intuition about chemical bonding.
Applying the variational method leads to two molecular orbitals (MOs) with distinct spatial character: one increases the electron probability density between the nuclei
respectively lower and raise the energy of the molecule relative to two separated atoms.
More accurate quantum chemical methods do not rely directly on atomic orbitals, but instead use abstract linear combinations of

children.alt: 1s atomic orbitals centered on each hydrogen atom

children.value: Fig. 1 The 1s atomic orbitals centered on each H atom in the

children.html: H2+H_2^+H2+​
H2+H_2^+H2+​

children.value: can be solved exactly because the system contains only a single electron. However, the analytical solution involves quite challenging mathematics. Instead, we adopt a simpler approximate approach that still captures the essential physics of chemical bonding.
We construct a trial (approximate) molecular wavefunction as a real linear combination of hydrogen 1s atomic orbitals:

1sA1s_A1sA​
1sB1s_B1sB​

children.value: are hydrogen 1s orbitals centered on nuclei A and B, respectively, and

c2c_2c2​

children.alt: Bonding versus antibonding wavefunctions and densities

children.value: Fig. 2 Bonding versus antibonding wavefunctions (molecular orbitals). Shown are the wavefunctions and the probability densities (squares of the wavefunctions).
The main feature of a chemical bond is the increased electron density between the nuclei. This identifies the

Energy of the hydrogen molecule ion

children.label: Energy of the hydrogen molecule ion

children.value: Using a linear combination of atomic orbitals, it is possible to calculate the best values, in terms of energy, of the coefficients

children.html: c1c_1c1​
c2c_2c2​

children.value: . Remember that this linear combination can only provide an approximate solution to the

children.html: H2+H_2^+H2+​

children.value: Schrodinger equation. The variational principle provides a systematic way to calculate the energy when

children.html: RRR

children.value: denote the integrals occurring in the variational treatment of the problem. The integrals

children.html: HAAH_{AA}HAA​
HBBH_{BB}HBB​

children.value: are called the Coulomb integrals (sometimes more generally termed matrix elements).
This interaction is attractive, and therefore its numerical value must be negative. Note that by symmetry

children.html: HAA=HBBH_{AA} = H_{BB}HAA​=HBB​
HABH_{AB}HAB​

children.value: is called the resonance integral, and also by symmetry

children.html: HAB=HBAH_{AB} = H_{BA}HAB​=HBA​

children.html: c1c_1c1​

children.value: , we calculate the partial derivatives of the energy with respect to these parameters:

children.html: c1c_1c1​
c2c_2c2​

children.value: ), the partial derivatives must be zero:

children.html: c1(HAA−E)+c2(HAB−SE)=0{c_1(H_{AA} - E) + c_2(H_{AB} - SE) = 0}c1​(HAA​−E)+c2​(HAB​−SE)=0
c2(HBB−E)+c1(HAB−SE)=0{c_2(H_{BB} - E) + c_1(H_{AB} - SE) = 0}c2​(HBB​−E)+c1​(HAB​−SE)=0

children.value: In matrix notation this is a generalized matrix eigenvalue problem:

children.html: (HAA−EHAB−SEHAB−SEHBB−E)(c1c2)=0{\begin{pmatrix}H_{AA} - E & H_{AB} - SE\\ H_{AB} - SE & H_{BB} - E\\ \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} c_1\\ c_2\\\end{pmatrix} = 0}(HAA​−EHAB​−SE​HAB​−SEHBB​−E​)(c1​c2​​)=0

children.value: From linear algebra, we know that a non-trivial solution exists only if the secular determinant vanishes:

children.html: ∣HAA−EHAB−SEHAB−SEHBB−E∣=0{\begin{vmatrix}H_{AA} - E & H_{AB} - SE\\ H_{AB} - SE & H_{BB} - E\\ \end{vmatrix} = 0}∣∣​HAA​−EHAB​−SE​HAB​−SEHBB​−E​∣∣​=0

children.alt: Energies of bonding and antibonding orbitals

children.value: Fig. 3 Energies of the bonding and antibonding orbitals as a function of internuclear distance.
Since energy is a relative quantity, it can be expressed relative to the separated nuclei:

children.html: ΔEg(R)=Eg(R)−E1s=J(R)+K(R)1+S(R){\Delta E_g(R) = E_g(R) - E_{1s} = \frac{J(R) + K(R)}{1 + S(R)}}ΔEg​(R)=Eg​(R)−E1s​=1+S(R)J(R)+K(R)​
ΔEu(R)=Eu(R)−E1s=J(R)−K(R)1−S(R){\Delta E_u(R) = E_u(R) - E_{1s} = \frac{J(R) - K(R)}{1 - S(R)}}ΔEu​(R)=Eu​(R)−E1s​=1−S(R)J(R)−K(R)​

children.value: Comparison of MO energies with experiment

children.label: Comparison of MO energies with experiment

children.value: These values can be compared with experimental results. The calculated ground state equilibrium bond length is 132 pm, whereas the experimental value is 106 pm. The binding energy is 170 kJ mol

children.value: It is common practice to represent the molecular orbitals using molecular orbital (MO) diagrams. The formation of bonding and antibonding orbitals can be visualized as follows:

children.value: Fig. 6 The MO diagram of homonuclear molecules follows a similar pattern with alternating bonding and antibonding MOs.

children.value: resulting from a calculation employing an extended basis set (more terms in the LCAO) are shown on the left below. The MO energy diagram, which includes the higher energy molecular orbitals, is shown on the right. Note that the energy order of the MOs depends on the molecule.
